text: The surgeon visually identifies a large endometrioma on the left side, noting its close proximity to another structure. The separation of the ovarian endometrioma reveals the invasion of the right endometrioma into the left endometrioma.
The surgeon trims the severely damaged right ovary, revealing paper-thin cortical tissue with no visible follicles. The lining is carefully pulled out for removal.

The surgeon demonstrates the paper-thin cortical tissue of the ovary, resembling the peeling of an onion, with minimal follicular presence. The lining is visibly stripped off, revealing another cyst in the surgical field.
The surgeon removes the lining from a larger cyst, revealing another cyst inside. The cyst is then opened up, exposing its contents.
The surgeon peels off and removes another cyst from the ovary, revealing the underlying tissue.

The camera focuses on the left ovary, revealing multiple follicles involved, indicating a direct invasion from the right ovary. The left ovary exhibits characteristics resembling a polycystic ovarian condition.

The surgeon attempts to manage small cysts on the ovary, highlighting the multiloculated appearance typical of a polycystic ovary. Traction and counter-traction techniques are employed to carefully manipulate the cysts.

The surgeon carefully dissects within the correct anatomical plane to avoid damage, revealing a small follicle from which chocolate-colored material is expelled. A larger cyst is extracted, showing multiloculation and the lining being removed. The remaining ovarian tissue appears abnormal with reduced volume, and several particles are visible at the bottom.

The surgeon examines the pelvic cavity, focusing on a subserosal broad-based myoma. The myoma is being pulled out, revealing its capsule, which was invaded by endometrioma. The ovaries appear closed and cleaned up.

The surgeon uses traction and counter-traction to pull out the myoma from the wide surgical bed, with visible oozing but no active bleeding. The vascular pedicle is trimmed, and the base of the myoma is closed with interrupted extracorporeal sutures. The pelvic cavity is shown with deep lesions in both parametral areas, and a rectal probe is visible.

The surgeon closes the posterior urinary wall to achieve hemostasis. The next step involves resecting deep lesions from the peritoneal side, where the ovary is attached. The peritoneum, which has deep lesions, is in close proximity to the ureter and urinary artery. Careful dissection of the ureters on each side is performed to prevent damage, as they are pulled toward the area of deep lesions.

The surgeon meticulously dissects the ureter, ensuring its direction is under direct observation. The dissection proceeds under the peritoneum to assess the depth of the lesion, avoiding excessive removal of tissue containing nerve fibers. The deep lesion is isolated, and the surgeon removes it while monitoring the fatty tissue to confirm clear margins.

The surgeon uses a rectal probe to examine the pelvic cavity, identifying a large peritoneal defect on the right side. The ureter is carefully dissected and identified, avoiding the hypogastric nerve and its branches. The perimetrium is pulled from the pelvic floor, revealing a cystic lesion approximately three-fourths of a centimeter in size, indicative of endometriotic cysts. The surgeon removes the cyst and the remaining deep lesion from the paramecium on the left side, resulting in a clean pelvic cavity.

The surgeon closes the posterior cul-de-sac and peritonealizes the area to eliminate dead space, preventing small bowel adhesion and obstruction. The ovaries are opened, revealing a peritoneal defect in the anterior cul-de-sac on the right side. A cystic lesion is identified and resected with clear margins, and the defective peritoneum is closed with a pursing suture. The surface lesion on the peritoneum is removed, and specimens are sent to pathology.
